title: Ajahn Lee's Keeping the Breath in Mind — The Body as Path
subtitle: The 5 breath stages as prāṇa-vāyu refinement; the 4 jhānas as samādhi stages; the body as the path

summary.one_line: Ajahn Lee Dhammadharo's breath meditation manual analyzed as a complete prāṇa-vāyu refinement system comparable to Layayoga nāḍī-śuddhi, with the 5 breath stages as a systematic method for jhāna at...
summary.scope: Covers Methods 1 and 2, the five basic steps, the seven basic steps, the four jhānas, nimitta phenomena, breath-as-body framework, and the body as direct path to nibbāna.

sources.label: Ajahn Lee Dhammadharo — Keeping the Breath in Mind & Lessons in Samādhi (Ṭhānissaro Bhikkhu trans.)

body.text: [See Keeping the Breath in Mind, Method 1, final summary. The five basic steps are: (1) eliminate bad preoccupations, (2) make the mind dwell on good preoccupations, (3) gather all good preoccupations into one — jhāna, (4) consider this one preoccupation as anicca/dukkha/anattā, (5) let all preoccupations follow their own nature. This is a complete prāṇāyāma curriculum: the five steps correspond to the five vāyus being refined and gathered into the central channel, culminating in the unfabricated property (asaṅkhata-dhātu), the cicchakti of pure knowing.]

[See Keeping the Breath in Mind, Method 2, steps 1-7. The seven steps form a systematic nāḍī-śuddhi: (1) long breaths with buddho, (2) clear awareness of each breath, (3) observing and adjusting the breath, spreading comfortable breath sensation down the spine and through the nerves, (4) learning four ways of adjusting the breath, (5) establishing focal points (tip of nose, middle of head, palate, throat, sternum, navel), (6) spreading awareness throughout the entire body, (7) uniting all breath sensations. The nerve-flow language maps directly onto the nāḍī system of Layayoga.]

[See Keeping the Breath in Mind, Method 1, the five bases: (1) tip of nose, (2) middle of forehead, (3) top of head, (4) middle of brain, (5) center of chest. These are the cakra-like stations through which the breath-prāṇa is refined. The nimitta (signs) — the brilliantly white ball or lump of light — is the manifestation of prāṇa condensed into a luminous form, comparable to the bindu in Layayoga. 'The bright white nimitta is useful to the body and mind: It is a pure breath that can cleanse the blood in the body, reducing or eliminating feelings of pain.']

[See Keeping the Breath in Mind, Introduction. The body — specifically the breath-body — is the path itself. 'From what I've observed in my own practice, there is only one path that is short, easy, effective and pleasant, and at the same time has hardly anything to lead you astray: the path of keeping the breath in mind.' This is the sruti of the body as direct means, equivalent to the Tantric doctrine that the body is the instrument of liberation. The breath is not merely a support for concentration; it is the vāhana (vehicle) through which the daimon — buddha, pure knowing — manifests.]

[See Keeping the Breath in Mind, Method 2, the four Noble Truths mapped onto breath. The five jhāna factors — vitakka (directed thought), vicāra (evaluation), pīti (rapture), sukha (pleasure), ekaggatārammaṇa (singleness of preoccupation) — are described as the 'beginning stage of Right Concentration.' The breath itself is the field in which all four Truths are directly seen: 'The in-and-out breath is stress [...] Not being aware of the breath as it goes in and out, not knowing the characteristics of the breath, is the cause of stress.' This is samādhi-prajñā, concentration and discernment as one movement.]

[See Keeping the Breath in Mind, Method 1, transcendent level. The mind as pure knowing (buddha) is the cicchakti, the consciousness-power that stands apart from both knowledge and the things known. 'Knowing doesn't arise and it doesn't fall away. This is santi-dhamma — the reality of peace. It knows goodness, but the knowing isn't goodness, and goodness isn't the knowing. It knows evil, but the knowing isn't evil, and evil isn't the knowing.' This is precisely the Tantric doctrine of cicchakti as the witness-consciousness (sākṣin) that is the daimon, the knower behind all knowing.]

issues.title: Missing comparison with Layayoga prāṇa-vāyu system
issues.description: The five vāyus of Layayoga (prāṇa, apāna, samāna, udāna, vyāna) should be mapped to the five breath stages described by Ajahn Lee.

versions.summary: Initial RO created. All 6 passages drafted from Ajahn Lee's text. Key themes: 5 breath stages, 7 basic steps, jhāna as samādhi, mind as cicchakti.
